summary: Gruber argues that Samsung's Galaxy Fold launch debacle reveals deep organizational dysfunction, not just a product failure. The device's folding screen broke after a day or two of normal use, and a required plastic screen protector was so poorly communicated that reviewers peeled it off. He contends that Samsung's engineering and quality control teams must have known the device wasn't ready, but were overruled by marketing's desire to be first to market with a foldable phone. He draws a parallel to Samsung's 2016 Note 7 battery fire recall as evidence of a pattern, and contrasts it with Apple's handling of the failed AirPower, which was never shipped despite being prematurely announced.
key_points: The Galaxy Fold's central feature — its folding screen — breaks after a day or two of normal use, making it fundamentally unfit for sale
Samsung's engineering and QC teams almost certainly knew the device wasn't ready, but the message was either ignored or didn't reach decision-makers
The Note 7 battery fire recall in 2016 establishes a pattern of Samsung shipping products with known critical defects
Apple's AirPower cancellation, while embarrassing, shows the difference between recognizing a design won't work and shipping it anyway
The dysfunction isn't about the idea of foldable phones — it's about organizational failures that allow broken products to reach customers
money_quotes: It's a folding phone that breaks at the hinge.
It's flatly ridiculous that they shipped these units to reviewers and intended to start selling them to customers this week.
Either they knew about the problems and went ahead with shipping anyway or they didn't know.
The more I think about it the more obvious it seems that at some level of the company, they knew the Fold didn't work.
At some point optimistic wishful thinking ('We'll figure this out') was replaced by cold hard reality ('This design isn't going to work').
The Galaxy Fold saga is not a funny story about a $2,000 gadget that didn't work. OK, you got me, it's not just a funny story about a $2,000 gadget that didn't work.
To function at all, it requires a plastic screen protector that numerous reviewers assumed was meant to be peeled off. And they decided that was fine.
key_insight: The Galaxy Fold's failure isn't a product story but an organizational one — Samsung's pattern of shipping fatally flawed flagship devices reveals a company where marketing overrides engineering and quality control warnings are ignored.
